Summary: This study proposes and evaluates three SVM classifiers using pre- and post-contrast T2 FLAIR and T1WI subtraction to differentiate treatment-related effects (TRE) from glioma recurrence. The results showed that the SVM classifier based on a combination of T1WI and T2 FLAIR subtraction maps was superior to the sole use of T1WI or T2 FLAIR for differentiating TRE from recurrence.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria are explicitly stated in the Materials and Methods section under 'Patients'.

Item3.reason: The reference standard for recurrence is pathological confirmation by second surgical resections, which is a high-quality standard. The clinical diagnosis criteria for TRE are also defined, including stability or regression without additional treatment, no progression on conventional MRI and MRS/MRP, and a minimum 6-month follow-up.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from two different centers, indicating a multi-center approach.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source (T1WI and T2 FLAIR) reflects established standardization approaches, as these are conventional MRI sequences widely used in clinical practice for glioma diagnosis and follow-up.

Item6.reason: The imaging protocol with acquisition parameters is described in the 'Imaging Protocol' section, including details about the scanners, sequences, voxel size, FOV, and contrast administration.

Item7.reason: The time interval between imaging and the reference standard is appropriate, as the imaging was performed when TRE or recurrence was first noticeable, and recurrence was confirmed by second surgical resections. The clinical criteria for TRE also include follow-up imaging to assess stability or regression.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of the lesions on the subtraction maps, as described in the 'Image Preprocessing and Segmentation' section. The regions of interest (ROIs) were manually segmented by neuroradiologists.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by neuroradiologists, not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The segmentation methodology is described, including the use of ITK-SNAP for manual segmentation by two neuroradiologists, followed by scrutiny by a neuroradiology professor. Intra- and interobserver reproducibility assessments were also conducted.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: Two neuroradiologists performed the segmentation, so it was not produced by a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features from the ROIs.

Item11.reason: The study mentions normalization of images by centering at the mean intensity and dividing by one standard deviation. Coregistration and subtraction are also performed. This suggests appropriate use of image preprocessing techniques with transparent description.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics for feature extraction, which is a standardized software. The URL is provided.

Item13.reason: The study describes the classes of features extracted (Shape, First Order, GLCM, GLSZ, GLRLM, NGTDM, GLDM). The parameters are set by default in pyradiomics.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features extracted from the images.

Condition5.reason: The study uses SVM, which is not an end-to-end deep learning approach.

Item14.reason: The study removes features based on Pearson correlation coefficient, which can be considered a method for removing non-robust features by assessing their stability and redundancy.

Item15.reason: The study uses recursive feature elimination (RFE) to select the best feature subsets, which is a method for dimensionality reduction by selecting informative features.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly justify the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size using statistical means or empirical evidence. While feature selection is performed, there's no discussion on whether the final number of features is appropriate for the sample size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that the dataset was randomly split into training and testing sets at a 7:3 ratio. This indicates a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study attempts to address potential confounding factors by using data from two different hospitals in China and taking measures to avoid multicenter MRI standardization bias. They used similar scanners and scan setups.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics for the classification task, such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV. ROC curves are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures by calculating 95% confidence intervals (CI) using a non-parametric bootstrap method.

Item22.reason: The study does not include a calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses both T1WI and T2 FLAIR, and does not provide a uni-parametric evaluation or proof of its inferiority. The authors do justify the use of both sequences by stating that T2 FLAIR provides complementary information to T1WI.

Item24.reason: The study does not compare the radiomic approach with a non-radiomic method representative of clinical practice. There is no comparison to semantic features or RADS/RECIST scoring.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the SVM model with a simple baseline reference model. There is no comparison to a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from two institutions.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data availability.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code availability.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention any model availability.
